parentQuestion: A patient is given an IV saline solution that is isotonic to blood. Why is isotonic saline used instead of pure water?

options: Movement of water across a semipermeable membrane
scaffolds.explain: OSMOSIS is the movement of WATER across a semipermeable membrane from low solute to high solute concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Osmosis specifically refers to water, not any molecule.
wrongExplains.2: Active transport uses ATP; osmosis is passive water movement.
wrongExplains.3: Gas diffusion is different; osmosis moves water.

options: Same solute concentration as the cell
scaffolds.explain: ISOTONIC means SAME SOLUTE CONCENTRATION as the cell. 'Iso' = same, 'tonic' = concentration. No net water movement.

wrongExplains.0: Higher solute is hypertonic; isotonic is equal.
wrongExplains.2: Lower solute is hypotonic; isotonic is equal.
wrongExplains.3: Isotonic solutions have solutes at the same concentration as cells.

scaffolds.q: What happens to cells in an isotonic solution?
options: They remain the same size (no net water movement)
scaffolds.explain: In an ISOTONIC solution, cells REMAIN THE SAME SIZE because there's no net water movement — concentrations are equal.

options: Lower solute concentration than the cell
Higher solute concentration than the cell
scaffolds.explain: HYPOTONIC means LOWER SOLUTE concentration than the cell. 'Hypo' = below. Water enters cells in hypotonic solutions.

wrongExplains.0: Same concentration is isotonic; hypotonic has less solute.
wrongExplains.2: Higher concentration is hypertonic; hypotonic has less.
wrongExplains.3: Hypotonic solutions contain water with low solute.

scaffolds.q: What happens to a cell in a hypotonic solution?
options: Water enters and it swells (may burst)
scaffolds.explain: In a HYPOTONIC solution, WATER ENTERS the cell and it SWELLS. If swelling is severe, the cell may burst (lyse).

wrongExplains.2: Osmosis causes water movement and cell volume changes.
wrongExplains.3: The cell swells, it doesn't just become isotonic.

scaffolds.q: Is pure water hypotonic to blood cells?
options: Yes, pure water has no solutes so it's hypotonic
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Pure water is HYPOTONIC to blood cells because it has NO SOLUTES. Water would rush into cells, causing them to swell and burst.

wrongExplains.0: Pure water has no solutes; it's not isotonic to cells.
wrongExplains.2: Hypertonic has more solute; pure water has none.
wrongExplains.3: Tonicity depends on solute concentration, not temperature.

scaffolds.explain: Normal (isotonic) saline is 0.9% NaCl — this matches the tonicity of blood and body fluids.

wrongExplains.0: 10% NaCl is hypertonic and would cause cell shrinkage.
wrongExplains.2: 0.1% NaCl is hypotonic and would cause cell swelling.
wrongExplains.3: 5% NaCl is hypertonic and would cause cell shrinkage.

options: Red blood cell bursting due to excess water
scaffolds.explain: HEMOLYSIS is RED BLOOD CELL BURSTING due to excess water entry (in hypotonic solutions). 'Hemo' = blood, 'lysis' = breaking.

wrongExplains.0: Shrinkage is crenation; hemolysis is bursting.
wrongExplains.2: Growth is enlargement; hemolysis is destruction.
wrongExplains.3: Clotting is coagulation; hemolysis is cell rupture.

scaffolds.q: Why is isotonic saline used instead of pure water for IV fluids?
options: Pure water is hypotonic and would cause red blood cells to swell and burst
scaffolds.explain: Pure water is HYPOTONIC and would cause RED BLOOD CELLS TO SWELL AND BURST (hemolysis). Isotonic saline matches blood tonicity, preventing this.

wrongExplains.0: The issue is osmotic damage, not cost.
wrongExplains.2: Taste is irrelevant; the issue is cell safety.
wrongExplains.3: There's a critical difference in osmotic effects.
